title: Recursion
description: Recursion is the foundation of tree traversals, divide-and-conquer, and backtracking. Master typed recursive functions, memoization with Map, and backtracking patterns to solve interview problems confidently.

sections.title: Typed recursive functions
sections.explanation: Every recursive function needs a base case and a recursive case. In TypeScript, annotate the return type to catch mistakes — if your recursion accidentally returns the wrong type, the compiler flags it. Start with simple examples like factorial and Fibonacci to internalize the pattern.

exercise.prompt: Write a typed recursive function `sumTo` that returns the sum from 1 to n. Write a typed recursive function `power` that computes base^exp. Print sumTo(10) and power(2, 8).

validation.message: Use typed recursion with explicit return types

exercise.hint: sumTo base case: n <= 1 returns n (sum of 1 is 1). power base case: exp === 0 returns 1 (anything to the 0th power is 1).

sections.title: Memoization with Map
sections.explanation: Naive recursion recalculates the same subproblems repeatedly. Memoization caches results in a `Map<number, number>` (or `Map<string, T>` for complex keys). Check the map before computing; store the result after. This converts exponential Fibonacci into O(n) — interviewers expect this optimization.

exercise.prompt: Write a memoized recursive function `climbStairs` that counts the number of ways to climb n stairs taking 1 or 2 steps at a time. Use a Map<number, number> for memoization. Print results for n=5 and n=10.

validation.message: Use Map-based memoization to cache results

exercise.hint: Climb n-1 (took 1 step) + climb n-2 (took 2 steps). memo.set(n, result) caches the answer.

sections.title: Recursive array processing
sections.explanation: Many interview problems use recursion to process arrays — binary search, merge sort, and generating subsets. The pattern is: base case for empty/single-element arrays, then recurse on subarrays. TypeScript's array types keep the recursion type-safe.

exercise.prompt: Write a recursive function `mergeSort` that sorts a number array. Split the array in half, sort each half recursively, then merge them. Print the sorted result for [5, 3, 8, 1, 9, 2].

validation.message: Use recursive divide-and-conquer with typed merge

exercise.hint: Split at mid: left half is slice(0, mid), right half is slice(mid). The merge function combines two sorted arrays.

sections.title: Backtracking
sections.explanation: Backtracking explores all candidates by choosing, recursing, then undoing the choice. In TypeScript, the result array is typed as `T[][]` and the current path as `T[]`. The pattern: if goal is met, push a copy of the path; otherwise loop through options, add to path, recurse, then pop. This solves combinations, permutations, and subset problems.

exercise.prompt: Write a backtracking function `combinations` that generates all combinations of k numbers from 1 to n. Print combinations(4, 2) — all 2-element combos from [1,2,3,4].

validation.message: Use typed backtracking with path copy and pop

exercise.hint: Base case: path.length === k. Recurse with i + 1 to avoid reusing elements. Pop after recursion to backtrack.

sections.title: Memoization with string keys
sections.explanation: When the recursive state has multiple parameters, serialize them into a string key for the memo Map. For grid problems, use `${row},${col}`. For string problems, use index pairs. This pattern is the bridge from recursion to dynamic programming.

exercise.prompt: Write a memoized function `minPathSum` that finds the minimum cost path from top-left to bottom-right in a grid (can only move right or down). Print the result for the given grid.

validation.message: Use string-key memoization for grid coordinates

exercise.hint: Move right: dp(r, c + 1). Move down: dp(r + 1, c). The key encodes both coordinates as a string.
